---
title: "Tutorials"
---

The tutorials combine explanation, maintained Python and C++ source code, and
output produced by pySEB during the documentation build.

## Choose a tutorial by task

| Tutorial | What you will learn | Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Diblock copolymer](diblock-copolymer.qmd) | Create an environment, install pySEB, add and link subunits, evaluate, and plot | Beginner |
| [Sphere–polymer micelle](micelle.qmd) | Attach tagged chains to distributed sphere-surface references | Intermediate |
| [Reusable nested structures](nested-structures.qmd) | Build reusable graphs and address nested reference paths | Intermediate |
| [Parameter sweeps](parameter-sweeps.qmd) | Reuse one graph with several parameter sets | Intermediate |
| [PDB cloud with an attached polymer](pdb-polymer.qmd) | Combine coordinate-based numerical scattering with an analytic subunit | Advanced |

## Advanced tutorials

| Tutorial | What you will learn |
| --- | --- |
| [Symbolic expressions and export](symbolic-expressions.qmd) | Select a backend, inspect expressions, convert to SymPy, and export Python, LaTeX, and C forms |
| [Scattering quantities](scattering-quantities.qmd) | Compare normalized and unnormalized form factors, amplitudes, phase factors, and size measures |
| [Figure 13 star chain](figure13-star-chain.qmd) | Construct and evaluate the published chain of five four-arm diblock stars |

Each task page presents Python and C++ in parallel. The Python blocks execute
in the documentation environment, the C++ listing comes from the maintained
source file, and its output comes from the compiled executable.

## Extended guides

[Symbolic and numerical scattering with Python](pyseb_tutorial.qmd) covers:

- analytic Gaussian-polymer structures;
- symbolic expressions and vector q evaluation;
- finite-sphere Debye clouds;
- callback-defined numerical subunits;
- PDB parsing and atom parameter profiles;
- mixed numerical and analytic structures.

[C++ modelling tutorials](cpp_tutorials.qmd) compile and run five C++14
programs:

1. a Gaussian diblock copolymer;
2. a sphere–polymer micelle;
3. reusable nested structures;
4. a parameter sweep written as CSV;
5. a PDB-derived Debye cloud with an attached polymer.

The displayed C++ is included directly from the compiled source files. The
terminal output beneath each program is captured from the executable during
the Quarto render.

## Before you begin

The diblock tutorial starts by creating a Python virtual environment and
installing pySEB with `pip`. To compile C++, use the
[platform build guides](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Build-Guides).
For terminology, reference syntax, and the available models, see the
[reference wiki](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki).
